whatIsCAGR.intro: CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate) is the annualized rate of growth in the value of an investment or financial metric over a stated period. It measures what an investment would yield on an annual basis, assuming the profits are reinvested at the end of each year.
whatIsCAGR.highlight: Unlike simple annual growth rates that short-term market fluctuations can influence, CAGR considers the compounding effect of returns, providing a more accurate measure of long-term growth.

smoothens.title: Smoothens volatility:
smoothens.text: CAGR effectively "smoothens" growth rates over multiple periods into a single annualized growth rate

comparison.title: Easy comparison:
comparison.text: Compare performance of different investments over varying time periods

compound.title: Compound effect:
compound.text: Takes into account the power of compounding, unlike simple averages

forecast.title: Future projections:
forecast.text: Useful for forecasting based on historical growth patterns

whatIsCAGR.keyInsightText: If you invested $10,000 and it grew to $20,000 over 5 years, the CAGR would be approximately 14.87% per year. This means your investment grew by an average of 14.87% annually over that period. Use our calculator above to find your investment's CAGR instantly.

formula.title: The CAGR Formula

fv.definition: The ending value of the investment

pv.definition: The beginning value or initial investment amount

n.definition: The investment time period in years

steps: Divide the final value (FV) by the initial value (PV)
Raise the result to the power of 1 divided by the number of years (n)
Multiply by 100 to convert to percentage

useCases.intro: CAGR is widely used across finance and business for various purposes. Our CAGR calculator helps you solve all these scenarios instantly. Here are the most common use cases:

cases.title: Investment Portfolio Growth
cases.description: Measure how your investment portfolio has performed over multiple years. For example, if your portfolio grew from $100,000 to $300,000 over 10 years, the CAGR of 11.6% shows your average annual growth rate.

cases.title: Investment Doubling Time
cases.description: Calculate how long it will take for your investment to double at a certain growth rate. If you invest $10,000 and want it to grow to $20,000, knowing your expected CAGR helps you estimate the timeframe.

cases.title: Company Revenue Analysis
cases.description: Analyze a company's revenue growth over multiple years to understand its long-term performance. This helps stakeholders make informed decisions about the company's financial health.

cases.title: Mutual Fund Comparison
cases.description: When selecting mutual funds, CAGR allows investors to compare the historical performance of different schemes over the same time period, providing an apples-to-apples comparison.
cases.example: Example: Compare Fund A (12% CAGR) vs Fund B (9% CAGR)

cases.title: Growth Forecasting
cases.description: Use historical CAGR to project future growth. If a business has maintained a 15% CAGR over the past 5 years, you can use this rate to forecast potential future performance.
cases.example: Example: Current value $50K at 15% CAGR = $100K in ~5 years

whenToUse: When you want to understand long-term investment performance
When comparing different investments over the same time period
When forecasting future growth based on historical trends
useCases.footer: Use our free online calculator above to perform any of these calculations instantly.

validationRules: Exactly 3 fields required: The calculator will automatically detect which variable to calculate
CAGR Rate format: Enter as percentage (e.g., 15 for 15%)
Positive values only: Initial and Final values must be greater than 0
Time period: Must be greater than 0 years (can be decimal, e.g., 2.5 years)
CAGR range: Between -100% and 1000% for practical calculations
interpretingItems: CAGR (%): Your annualized growth rate - higher is better (but consider risk)
Total Growth (%): The overall percentage increase from start to end
Absolute Return ($): The actual dollar amount gained or lost
Doubling Time: How long it takes for your money to double at this growth rate
Benchmark Comparison: How your result compares to market averages (S&P 500, bonds, etc.)

faq.intro: Here are answers to the most common questions about CAGR and our free online CAGR calculator:

questions.question: What is the difference between CAGR and average return?
questions.answer: CAGR is a geometric mean that accounts for compounding, while average return is an arithmetic mean. CAGR is more accurate for measuring investment performance over time because it reflects the actual growth rate if returns were compounded annually.

questions.question: Can CAGR be negative?
questions.answer: Yes, CAGR can be negative if the final value is less than the initial value. A negative CAGR indicates that the investment has lost value over the time period. For example, if $10,000 becomes $8,000 over 5 years, the CAGR would be approximately -4.4%.

questions.question: Is higher CAGR always better?
questions.answer: Not necessarily. While higher CAGR indicates faster growth, it doesn't account for risk or volatility. An investment with 25% CAGR but high volatility might be riskier than one with 15% CAGR and stable growth. Always consider CAGR alongside other metrics like volatility, Sharpe ratio, and maximum drawdown.

questions.question: How accurate is CAGR for short periods?
questions.answer: CAGR can be misleading for very short periods (less than 1 year) because it annualizes the returns, which can exaggerate or distort the actual performance. For periods shorter than a year, absolute return is often more appropriate.

questions.question: When should I use CAGR vs absolute return?
questions.answer: Use CAGR when comparing investments over different time periods or when you want to understand annualized growth rates (typically 3+ years). Use absolute return for shorter periods, single-period performance, or when the time period is less relevant to your analysis.

questions.question: Does CAGR account for volatility?
questions.answer: No, CAGR does not account for volatility. It only shows the smoothed growth rate from start to end, ignoring the ups and downs along the way. Two investments can have the same CAGR but vastly different volatility profiles. Consider using additional metrics like standard deviation or Sharpe ratio to assess risk.

questions.question: Can I use CAGR for monthly data?
questions.answer: Yes, but you need to adjust the time period accordingly. If you have monthly data over 3 years, use n = 36 months instead of 3 years. The formula remains the same: (FV/PV)^(1/n) - 1, but remember to annualize the result by multiplying by 12 if you want a yearly rate.

questions.question: What's a good CAGR for stocks, bonds, and real estate?
questions.answer: Historically: S&P 500 stocks ~10-11% CAGR, Corporate bonds ~5-6% CAGR, Real estate ~8-9% CAGR, and Gold ~7% CAGR. However, past performance doesn't guarantee future returns. A "good" CAGR depends on your risk tolerance, investment goals, and the broader economic context.

questions.question: How do I calculate CAGR in Excel?
questions.answer: In Excel, use this formula: =(FV/PV)^(1/n)-1 or =POWER(FV/PV, 1/n)-1. You can also use the RATE function: =RATE(n, 0, -PV, FV). For example, for $10,000 growing to $20,000 over 5 years: =POWER(20000/10000, 1/5)-1 returns 0.1487 or 14.87%. However, our free online calculator is much faster and eliminates the risk of formula errors.

questions.question: What are the main limitations of CAGR?
questions.answer: CAGR assumes constant growth, which rarely occurs in reality. It doesn't account for volatility, risk, or intermediate cash flows (dividends, deposits, withdrawals). It also doesn't reflect the timing of returns—you could have negative returns for 4 years and a spike in year 5, still showing a positive CAGR. Always use CAGR alongside other metrics for a complete picture.

faq.stillHaveQuestionsText: This calculator is designed to be as accurate and helpful as possible. All calculations are based on widely-accepted financial formulas. If you have additional questions about CAGR or need help interpreting your results, consider consulting with a financial advisor.

about.technologyIntro: Built with modern web technologies to ensure fast performance and excellent user experience:

cagrVsMetrics.intro: While CAGR is a powerful metric, it's important to understand how it compares to other commonly used financial metrics. Each has its strengths and limitations.

metrics.name: CAGR
metrics.description: Smoothed annualized growth rate assuming compounding

metrics.name: Simple Average Return
metrics.description: Arithmetic mean of annual returns (can be misleading)

metrics.name: Absolute Return
metrics.description: Total percentage change from start to end

metrics.name: IRR (Internal Rate of Return)
metrics.description: Accounts for timing of cash flows in/out

metrics.name: TWR (Time-Weighted Return)
metrics.description: Removes impact of external cash flows

advantages: Not distorted by extreme percentage changes
limitations: Doesn't account for volatility or risk
Can be misleading for very short periods
Past CAGR doesn't guarantee future returns
cagrVsMetrics.exampleIntro: Consider an investment that goes: $100 → $150 → $120 → $180 over 3 years
cagrVsMetrics.exampleNotice: Notice: CAGR (21.7%) is more accurate because it accounts for compounding, while the simple average (26.67%) overestimates the true annualized growth.
